text:
COMPREHENSIVE GENOMIC PROFILING REPORT
Next-Generation Sequencing — Solid Tumor Panel (523 Genes)

================================================================================
PATIENT & SPECIMEN INFORMATION
================================================================================
Patient Name: Margaret Osei-Bonsu
Date of Birth: [REDACTED — age 52 at time of report]
Sex: Female
Ordering Physician: [Head and Neck Medical Oncology]
Diagnosis (per requisition): Squamous cell carcinoma, base of tongue, p16-positive, HPV-associated
Primary Site: Right base of tongue (ICD-O: C01.9; Histology: 8070/3)
Clinical Stage: T2N1M0, Stage III (AJCC 8th edition conventional); Stage I by HPV-positive oropharyngeal SCC staging criteria

Specimen ID: [LAB-2453-BTNG-001]
Specimen Type: Formalin-fixed, paraffin-embedded (FFPE) tumor tissue — right base of tongue, direct laryngoscopy biopsy
Tumor Cellularity (pathology review): 65% (adequate for sequencing)
Nucleic Acid QC: DNA yield 420 ng; RNA yield 310 ng; OD 260/280 = 1.89; DNA integrity adequate for library preparation
Sequencing Platform: Illumina NovaSeq 6000
Panel: Comprehensive solid tumor panel — 523 genes (full coding exons, selected introns, copy number, TMB, MSI, fusion detection)
Mean Coverage Depth: 847x (tumor); 312x (matched normal buffy coat)
Analysis: Somatic variant calling with matched normal subtraction for germline filtering

================================================================================
CLINICAL SUMMARY
================================================================================
This is a 52-year-old female, never-smoker of West African descent, with newly diagnosed p16-positive/HPV-associated squamous cell carcinoma of the right base of tongue (T2N1M0). Tumor measured 3.2 cm at primary site with a single ipsilateral right level IIA lymph node measuring 2.1 cm. HPV RNA in situ hybridization confirmed transcriptionally active high-risk HPV type 16. EBER negative. Prior to sequencing, PD-L1 (SP142 clone) CPS was reported at 12 by the pathology laboratory. No prior systemic therapy. This report presents somatic genomic alterations identified in the tumor tissue, with integrated matched germline analysis.

================================================================================
GENOMIC FINDINGS — SOMATIC ALTERATIONS
================================================================================

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
SECTION 1: PATHOGENIC / LIKELY PATHOGENIC VARIANTS (CLINICALLY ACTIONABLE)
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

1. PIK3CA c.1633G>A, p.Glu545Lys (E545K)
   Gene Function: Phosphatidylinositol-4,5-bisphosphate 3-kinase catalytic subunit alpha (PIK3CA); encodes the catalytic p110-alpha subunit of PI3K. Activating mutations drive downstream AKT/mTOR signaling.
   Classification: PATHOGENIC — Tier I (well-established oncogenic driver, known hotspot)
   Variant Type: Missense substitution, exon 9 (helical domain)
   Allele Frequency (tumor): 18.2%
   Coverage at locus: 914x tumor / 289x normal
   Germline status: Not detected in matched normal (somatic)
   Functional impact: E545K is one of the two most common PIK3CA hotspot mutations (alongside E542K and H1047R). Located in the helical domain, this mutation disrupts an inhibitory interaction between the helical and regulatory domains, resulting in constitutive kinase activation and downstream PI3K/AKT/mTOR pathway upregulation. This alteration is well-described in HPV-associated oropharyngeal SCC (OPSCC), head and neck SCC broadly, breast, colorectal, and other epithelial malignancies.
   Clinical relevance: PIK3CA E545K is a known activating oncogenic driver. In the context of HPV-positive OPSCC, PIK3CA mutations occur in approximately 20-30% of cases and are associated with PI3K pathway dependency. Alpelisib (a PI3K-alpha selective inhibitor, FDA-approved in PIK3CA-mutant breast cancer) and other PI3K/mTOR pathway inhibitors represent potential therapeutic considerations in the recurrent/metastatic setting. Available clinical trials targeting PIK3CA-mutant HNSCC should be considered if disease recurs.

2. TERT Promoter c.-124C>T (C228T)
   Gene Function: Telomerase reverse transcriptase (TERT); core catalytic subunit of telomerase. Promoter mutations create de novo binding sites for ETS transcription factors, leading to increased TERT expression and telomere maintenance — enabling replicative immortality.
   Classification: PATHOGENIC — Tier I (well-established oncogenic driver, recurrent promoter hotspot)
   Variant Type: Promoter region mutation (C228T), non-coding
   Allele Frequency (tumor): 22.1%
   Coverage at locus: 987x tumor / 301x normal
   Germline status: Not detected in matched normal (somatic)
   Functional impact: The TERT promoter C228T mutation (also denoted -124C>T) is one of the two canonical TERT promoter hotspot mutations (alongside C250T). These mutations are among the most prevalent somatic mutations across human cancers and are highly recurrent in HNSCC, thyroid carcinoma, glioma, melanoma, and urothelial carcinoma. In HNSCC, TERT promoter mutations co-occur frequently with other alterations driving cell cycle dysregulation.
   Clinical relevance: No direct approved targeted therapy. In HNSCC, TERT promoter mutations have been associated with adverse outcomes in some HPV-negative cohorts but have less established prognostic significance in HPV-positive OPSCC. This alteration is noted for completeness and potential future clinical trial eligibility.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
SECTION 2: LIKELY PATHOGENIC VARIANTS
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

3. FAT1 c.7201C>T, p.Gln2401Ter (Q2401*)
   Gene Function: FAT1 is a protocadherin-type tumor suppressor gene on chromosome 4q35. It modulates Wnt/beta-catenin signaling, Hippo pathway activity (YAP/TAZ regulation), and actin cytoskeleton dynamics. Loss-of-function FAT1 mutations disinhibit Wnt and Hippo pathway outputs, promoting invasion and proliferation.
   Classification: LIKELY PATHOGENIC — Tier II (recurrent loss-of-function mechanism in HNSCC)
   Variant Type: Nonsense mutation (premature stop codon), exon 27
   Allele Frequency (tumor): 14.9%
   Coverage at locus: 876x tumor / 278x normal
   Germline status: Not detected in matched normal (somatic)
   Functional impact: FAT1 is among the most frequently mutated genes in HNSCC overall (mutated in ~20-30% of cases across HPV-positive and HPV-negative subtypes). Nonsense and frameshift mutations result in loss-of-function alleles with predicted haploinsufficiency or complete loss when combined with LOH. The Q2401* mutation truncates the intracellular domain critical for beta-catenin sequestration and Hippo pathway co-receptor function.
   Clinical relevance: FAT1 loss-of-function has been associated with sensitivity to Wnt pathway inhibitors in preclinical models. No FDA-approved direct targeted therapies. FAT1 alteration is noted for completeness and potential eligibility for investigational trials.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
SECTION 3: VARIANTS OF UNCERTAIN SIGNIFICANCE (VUS)
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

4. EP300 c.4880G>A, p.Arg1627Gln (R1627Q)
   Gene Function: EP300 (E1A binding protein p300) encodes a histone acetyltransferase (HAT) and transcriptional co-activator. It functions as a tumor suppressor in squamous cell carcinomas through regulation of p53 transactivation, chromatin remodeling, and cell cycle control.
   Classification: VARIANT OF UNCERTAIN SIGNIFICANCE (VUS)
   Variant Type: Missense substitution, exon 31 (HAT domain adjacent)
   Allele Frequency (tumor): 11.3%
   Coverage at locus: 845x tumor / 291x normal
   Germline status: Not detected in matched normal (somatic)
   Functional impact: EP300 mutations are recurrent in HNSCC (approximately 10-15% of cases) and frequently occur in the HAT or bromodomain regions. The R1627Q substitution in the HAT-adjacent region has not been functionally characterized in published literature. In silico prediction tools (SIFT: Tolerated; PolyPhen-2: Possibly damaging; REVEL score: 0.42) are discordant, preventing definitive classification. This variant is classified as a VUS pending functional validation studies or accumulation of additional evidence.
   Clinical relevance: EP300 loss-of-function has been associated with sensitivity to HDAC inhibitors (e.g., vorinostat, romidepsin) in preclinical models. No direct clinical actionability at this time.

5. NOTCH1 c.1393G>A, p.Ala465Thr (A465T)
   Gene Function: NOTCH1 encodes a transmembrane receptor governing cell fate, differentiation, and survival. In squamous epithelium, NOTCH1 typically functions as a tumor suppressor by promoting keratinocyte terminal differentiation. Loss-of-function NOTCH1 mutations are frequently found in HNSCC and cutaneous SCC.
   Classification: VARIANT OF UNCERTAIN SIGNIFICANCE (VUS)
   Variant Type: Missense substitution, exon 9 (EGF-like repeat domain)
   Allele Frequency (tumor): 9.1%
   Coverage at locus: 823x tumor / 283x normal
   Germline status: Not detected in matched normal (somatic)
   Functional impact: NOTCH1 is mutated in approximately 14-20% of HNSCC. Missense mutations in the EGF-like repeat extracellular domain can impair ligand binding and downstream signaling; however, the A465T substitution has not been characterized as a recurrent hotspot in published HNSCC databases (COSMIC, TCGA). In silico tools (SIFT: Tolerated; PolyPhen-2: Benign; REVEL score: 0.21) suggest low functional impact, and this alteration is classified as a VUS pending additional evidence.
   Clinical relevance: No direct clinical actionability.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
SECTION 4: COPY NUMBER ALTERATIONS (CNA)
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

6. CCND1 Amplification
   Chromosomal Location: 11q13.3
   Estimated Copy Number: 6.4 copies (fold amplification above diploid)
   Classification: PATHOGENIC (amplification of established oncogene)
   Functional impact: CCND1 (Cyclin D1) amplification results in overexpression of cyclin D1, which complexes with CDK4/6 to phosphorylate Rb and drive G1/S cell cycle progression. CCND1 amplification at 11q13 is one of the most prevalent copy number alterations in HNSCC, occurring in approximately 30-40% of cases. In HPV-positive OPSCC, CCND1 amplification can co-occur alongside HPV-mediated p16 upregulation (a paradox of pathway redundancy); however, CCND1 amplification in this context may partially offset the growth-inhibitory effect of p16 overexpression.
   Co-occurring alteration note: CCND1 amplification co-occurring with CDKN2A homozygous deletion (see below) represents a dual mechanism of CDK4/6 pathway hyperactivation — accelerating cell cycle entry via simultaneous cyclin D1 gain and p16 loss. This co-occurrence is biologically consistent and has been described in HNSCC genomic studies.
   Clinical relevance: CDK4/6 inhibitors (palbociclib, ribociclib, abemaciclib) have demonstrated activity in CCND1-amplified solid tumors including HNSCC in early-phase trials. No FDA approval currently exists for CCND1-amplified HNSCC outside of clinical trial settings.

7. CDKN2A Homozygous Deletion
   Chromosomal Location: 9p21.3
   Estimated Copy Number: 0 copies (homozygous deletion)
   Classification: PATHOGENIC (loss of established tumor suppressor)
   Functional impact: CDKN2A encodes p16(INK4A) and p14(ARF). Homozygous deletion results in complete loss of both p16 and p14ARF expression. p16 normally inhibits CDK4/6-mediated phosphorylation of Rb, acting as a cell cycle brake. p14ARF stabilizes p53 by inhibiting MDM2. Complete CDKN2A loss therefore simultaneously dysregulates both the Rb and p53 pathways.
   Interpretive note regarding IHC p16 positivity: It is important to distinguish between p16 protein expression detected by immunohistochemistry (a surrogate marker for transcriptionally active HPV infection) and genomic CDKN2A status. In HPV-associated OPSCC, p16 IHC overexpression reflects HPV E7 oncoprotein-mediated upregulation of p16 transcription (a feedback response to Rb inactivation by E7), not intact p16 protein function. Therefore, p16 IHC positivity can paradoxically coexist with homozygous CDKN2A deletion in a subset of HPV-positive OPSCC cases; the HPV-driven pathway mediates Rb suppression through E7 (rendering p16 feedback irrelevant), while the genomic deletion eliminates any residual p16/p14ARF tumor suppressive function. This interpretation is consistent with published observations in the TCGA HNSCC cohort.
   Clinical relevance: CDKN2A deletion identifies potential sensitivity to CDK4/6 inhibitor therapy (when combined with CCND1 amplification, as in this tumor). Additionally, loss of p14ARF can confer relative p53 pathway impairment. No FDA-approved therapy specifically indicated by CDKN2A deletion in HNSCC.

8. EGFR Copy Number Gain (Low-Level)
   Chromosomal Location: 7p11.2
   Estimated Copy Number: 3.1 copies (low-level polysomy/gain, not high-level amplification)
   Classification: LIKELY PATHOGENIC — LOW-LEVEL GAIN (significance uncertain at this copy number threshold)
   Functional impact: EGFR (Epidermal Growth Factor Receptor) overexpression/copy number gain is among the most prevalent molecular alterations in HNSCC (occurring in up to 80-90% of all HNSCC cases at the protein level). Low-level copy number gain (3-4 copies) is distinguished from true high-level amplification (>6 copies or focal high-level amplification) and is associated with modest receptor overexpression. EGFR is the target of cetuximab (chimeric anti-EGFR monoclonal antibody) approved for HNSCC.
   Note: No EGFR exon 19 (del19) or exon 21 (L858R) activating point mutations were detected. No EGFR exon 20 insertions detected. The copy number gain represents a quantitative alteration only.
   Clinical relevance: Cetuximab is approved for HNSCC, but EGFR copy number alone has not been established as a robust predictive biomarker for cetuximab benefit in HNSCC at the level seen with EGFR-activating mutations in NSCLC. Low-level gain at this threshold does not independently mandate anti-EGFR therapy.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
SECTION 5: NEGATIVE / NOT DETECTED FINDINGS (CLINICALLY SIGNIFICANT NEGATIVES)
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

The following alterations were evaluated and NOT DETECTED:

- TP53 mutation: NOT DETECTED
  Note: Absence of TP53 mutation is the expected finding in HPV-positive oropharyngeal SCC and is a hallmark of HPV-driven oncogenesis. HPV E6 oncoprotein mediates ubiquitin-dependent proteasomal degradation of p53, functionally inactivating the p53 pathway without requiring somatic TP53 mutation. This is in contrast to HPV-negative HNSCC, where TP53 mutation rates exceed 80%.

- KRAS, NRAS, HRAS mutations: NOT DETECTED
  RAS pathway mutations are infrequent in HNSCC and their absence is expected in this tumor type and biology.

- BRAF mutations (including V600E): NOT DETECTED

- ALK rearrangement/fusion: NOT DETECTED (IHC and RNA-based fusion analysis)

- RET rearrangement/fusion: NOT DETECTED

- NTRK1, NTRK2, NTRK3 fusions: NOT DETECTED
  Note: NTRK fusions are rare in HNSCC but represent a pan-tumor actionable target (larotrectinib, entrectinib). Absence confirmed.

- ROS1 rearrangement/fusion: NOT DETECTED

- FGFR2 amplification/mutation/fusion: NOT DETECTED

- FGFR3 amplification/mutation/fusion: NOT DETECTED

- MET amplification/exon 14 skipping mutation: NOT DETECTED

- ERBB2 (HER2) amplification or activating mutation: NOT DETECTED

- CDKN2B deletion (9p21.3): Not detected as independent event; CDKN2A deletion region does not span CDKN2B locus on available resolution.

- MDM2 amplification: NOT DETECTED

- CCNE1 amplification: NOT DETECTED

- STK11 mutation: NOT DETECTED

- KEAP1/NFE2L2 mutations: NOT DETECTED

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
SECTION 6: VIRAL INTEGRATION ANALYSIS
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

HPV Integration Status: DETECTED
HPV Subtype: HPV-16 (confirmed by sequencing reads mapping to HPV-16 reference genome)
Integration Site(s): Chromosome 3q26 (integration junction identified near PIK3CA locus; additional junction at chromosome 8q24); disruption of DNER locus at chr2p14 (secondary integration site, lower confidence)
Viral Load (tumor-derived reads): 12.4% of total sequencing reads map to HPV-16 genome
Viral genome status: Evidence of E2 region disruption (consistent with episomal-to-integrated transition and loss of E2-mediated E6/E7 repression)
EBER (EBV): Not detected (confirmed negative; consistent with IHC/ISH negativity)

Interpretive note: HPV-16 integration near the PIK3CA locus (3q26) is a recurrent integration pattern reported in HPV-positive OPSCC and cervical carcinoma. Integration at 3q26 can contribute to amplification of the surrounding chromosomal region. The co-occurrence of a PIK3CA hotspot point mutation (E545K, AF 18%) and HPV integration in this region may reflect convergent mechanisms of PI3K pathway activation.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
SECTION 7: BIOMARKER SUMMARY
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Tumor Mutational Burden (TMB):
  Value: 6.2 mutations/megabase (mutations/Mb)
  Classification: LOW (TMB-Low; below 10 mutations/Mb threshold)
  Methodology: Non-synonymous somatic mutations per megabase of coding sequence, filtered for germline variants using matched normal
  Clinical relevance: TMB-Low status does not support TMB-based pembrolizumab eligibility per FDA-approved indication (TMB-H defined as >=10 mut/Mb). Pembrolizumab retains eligibility based on PD-L1 CPS >= 1 for recurrent/metastatic HNSCC. TMB-Low in HPV-positive OPSCC is an expected finding; HPV-positive tumors typically demonstrate low TMB relative to HPV-negative tobacco-associated HNSCC (which can approach 10-20+ mutations/Mb).

Microsatellite Instability (MSI):
  Status: MICROSATELLITE STABLE (MSS)
  Methodology: Analysis of 3,000+ microsatellite loci across the genome; instability fraction: 0.4% (threshold for MSI-High: >3.5%)
  Clinical relevance: MSS status does not support eligibility for MSI-H-indicated pembrolizumab or dostarlimab. Expected finding in HNSCC.

PD-L1 Expression:
  CPS (Combined Positive Score, SP142 antibody): 12
  Classification: PD-L1 positive (CPS >= 1)
  Note: PD-L1 CPS 12 is above the threshold for pembrolizumab eligibility in recurrent/metastatic HNSCC per KEYNOTE-048 criteria. In the definitive concurrent chemoradiation setting, PD-L1 status is currently not used for treatment selection per standard of care.

================================================================================
GERMLINE ANALYSIS — INCIDENTAL / SECONDARY FINDINGS
================================================================================

Germline Variant Identified (Matched Normal Analysis):

BRCA2 c.8167G>C, p.Asp2723His (D2723H)
  Allele Frequency (germline): 48.1% (consistent with heterozygous germline variant)
  Coverage: 312x (normal buffy coat)
  Classification: VARIANT OF UNCERTAIN SIGNIFICANCE (VUS) — germline
  Population frequency: gnomAD v4.0: 0.00041 (allele frequency); observed predominantly in individuals of African/West African ancestry
  Functional data: ClinVar: Conflicting interpretations (2 VUS submissions, 1 likely benign submission). No homologous recombination functional assay data available in public databases. REVEL score: 0.38 (low predicted functional impact). Evolutionary conservation: moderately conserved position.
  Interpretive note: The allele frequency of 48% in the matched normal blood specimen is strongly consistent with a constitutional (germline) heterozygous variant. The population frequency in gnomAD, with enrichment in African-ancestry populations, suggests this variant may represent a population-specific polymorphism rather than a pathogenic BRCA2 mutation. However, given the known variable penetrance of BRCA2 VUS and the patient's West African ancestry, formal genetic counseling is warranted.
  Recommendation: Referral to certified genetic counselor for formal germline BRCA2 VUS interpretation, family variant testing considerations, and discussion of clinical implications. Cascade testing of first-degree relatives may be considered at the discretion of the genetic counselor. This variant does not meet criteria for PARP inhibitor eligibility at this time.

Additional germline pathogenic/likely pathogenic variants in cancer predisposition genes (ATM, CHEK2, PALB2, RAD51C, RAD51D, MLH1, MSH2, MSH6, PMS2, APC, MUTYH, TP53, CDH1, STK11, PTEN, RB1, VHL, MEN1, RET, SDHA/B/C/D, MAX, TMEM127): NOT DETECTED

================================================================================
INTEGRATED GENOMIC INTERPRETATION
================================================================================

This HPV-positive oropharyngeal squamous cell carcinoma demonstrates a molecular profile characteristic of HPV-driven oncogenesis with several co-occurring genomic alterations typical of this tumor subtype.

Oncogenic pathway activation summary:

1. PI3K/AKT/mTOR Pathway (HIGH-LEVEL ACTIVATION):
   PIK3CA E545K activating mutation (somatic, AF 18%) + HPV integration near PIK3CA locus (3q26) represent convergent mechanisms of PI3K pathway hyperactivation. This pathway is among the most commonly altered in HPV-positive OPSCC and represents the primary actionable target identified in this tumor.

2. Cell Cycle / Rb Pathway (HIGH-LEVEL DISRUPTION):
   CCND1 amplification (6.4-fold) + CDKN2A homozygous deletion + HPV E7-mediated Rb sequestration represent triple convergent cell cycle dysregulation. This degree of Rb pathway disruption is notable and may have implications for CDK4/6 inhibitor sensitivity (palbociclib, abemaciclib) in recurrent/metastatic settings.

3. Telomere Maintenance (ACTIVATED):
   TERT promoter C228T mutation enables replicative immortality via telomerase upregulation.

4. Chromatin / Epigenetic Regulation (POTENTIALLY DISRUPTED):
   EP300 missense mutation (VUS) at AF 11% may contribute to altered histone acetylation patterns, though functional significance is uncertain.

5. Notch / Wnt / Cell Adhesion (POTENTIALLY DISRUPTED):
   FAT1 nonsense mutation (Q2401*, likely pathogenic) represents loss of Hippo/Wnt pathway tumor suppressor function.
   NOTCH1 missense mutation (A465T, VUS) may contribute to NOTCH pathway dysregulation, though functional impact is uncertain.

6. HPV Oncogene-Mediated Pathway Suppression:
   HPV-16 E6 protein mediates p53 ubiquitination and degradation → functional p53 loss without TP53 somatic mutation (confirmed: TP53 not mutated).
   HPV-16 E7 protein binds and inactivates Rb → functional Rb loss contributing to cell cycle dysregulation (compounded by CDKN2A deletion and CCND1 amplification as above).

EGFR copy number gain (low-level, 3.1 copies) is a common background finding in HNSCC and does not alter clinical management independently of the primary actionable findings.

The overall molecular landscape — HPV-positive status, absent TP53 mutation, PI3K pathway activation, and Rb/cell cycle pathway disruption — is entirely consistent with published HPV-positive OPSCC genomics (TCGA, ORIEN, HNSCC-specific studies). TMB-Low and MSS status are expected. PD-L1 CPS 12 confirms PD-1 pathway expression.

================================================================================
CLINICAL ACTIONABILITY SUMMARY TABLE
================================================================================

Alteration            | Classification    | Pathway           | Tier | Actionability
------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
PIK3CA E545K          | Pathogenic        | PI3K/AKT/mTOR     | I    | Actionable — PI3K inhibitors (investigational in HNSCC); alpelisib FDA-approved in PIK3CA-mutant breast cancer; clinical trial eligibility
TERT C228T            | Pathogenic        | Telomere maint.   | I    | No direct approved therapy; trial eligibility
FAT1 Q2401*           | Likely Pathogenic | Wnt/Hippo         | II   | No direct approved therapy
CCND1 Amplification   | Pathogenic        | Cell cycle/CDK4/6 | I    | CDK4/6 inhibitors (investigational in HNSCC)
CDKN2A Deletion       | Pathogenic        | Cell cycle/Rb     | I    | CDK4/6 inhibitors (investigational)
EGFR Copy Number Gain | Likely Path.      | EGFR/RAS/MAPK     | II   | Cetuximab (EGFR-approved in HNSCC; gain not independently predictive)
EP300 R1627Q          | VUS               | Chromatin/HAT     | III  | Not actionable
NOTCH1 A465T          | VUS               | Notch             | III  | Not actionable
PD-L1 CPS 12          | Biomarker         | Immune checkpoint | I    | Pembrolizumab eligible (recurrent/met setting)
TMB 6.2 mut/Mb        | Low               | Immunotherapy     | —    | Not eligible for TMB-H pembrolizumab indication
MSS                   | Negative          | Immunotherapy     | —    | Not eligible for MSI-H immunotherapy indication
BRCA2 D2723H (germ)  | Germline VUS      | HRR               | —    | Genetic counseling recommended; PARP inhibitor not indicated

================================================================================
QUALITY METRICS
================================================================================
Mean tumor coverage depth: 847x
Percent of targeted bases >100x: 99.7%
Percent of targeted bases >500x: 94.3%
Tumor fraction (bioinformatic estimate): 62%
Signal-to-noise ratio: 18.4 (adequate)
Library preparation: Hybridization capture, dual-indexed
Sequencing error rate: 0.08%
Bioinformatics pipeline: somatic SNV/indel (mutect2 + custom filtering), CNA (CNVkit), MSI (MSISensor2), TMB (custom coding sequence calculation), fusion (STAR-Fusion, Arriba)
All variants confirmed by orthogonal sequencing at > 50x depth at variant locus.
This report meets laboratory quality standards. No QC failures.

================================================================================
METHODOLOGY NOTE
================================================================================
This assay interrogates 523 cancer-relevant genes for somatic single nucleotide variants, small insertions/deletions (indels), copy number alterations, and selected structural variants/fusions. Matched normal (peripheral blood) sequencing was performed for germline subtraction. Variants with allele frequencies < 5% were filtered unless supported by > 20 variant-supporting reads. Variants in regions of known technical artifact, segmental duplication, or low mappability were excluded. Copy number calls require confirmation by FISH or additional methods for clinical decision-making in ambiguous cases. This assay is not validated for detection of constitutional mismatch repair deficiency (Lynch syndrome); separate germline MMR testing may be required if clinically indicated. TERT promoter and other non-coding regions are captured by custom probe tiling. HPV integration analysis is performed using alignment of unmapped reads to viral reference sequences with integration junction identification. This laboratory report is for research and clinical use by the treating physician; it does not constitute independent medical advice.

================================================================================
ATTENDING MOLECULAR PATHOLOGIST SIGN-OUT
================================================================================
Report reviewed and signed by: [Molecular Pathologist, MD, PhD — Board Certified in Clinical Molecular Genetics]
CAP/CLIA Accredited Laboratory
Report Status: FINAL

================================================================================
END OF REPORT
================================================================================
